paragraphs.text: When the famine seized also the house of Jacob, Jacob said to his sons: “Fear not; behold, I have heard that there is corn in Egypt; get you down thither, and buy… that we may live, and not die” (Gen 42:2). Jacob’s saying, “Fear not,” shows that his sons were indeed greatly afraid. The words, “I have heard that there is corn in Egypt,” show that in all the land they had no bread. And the words, “buy for us… that we may live, and not die,” bear witness to the need which Jacob’s sons suffered from the famine along with all the land of Canaan.

“And the brothers came to Joseph, and bowed themselves down to him with their faces to the earth” (Gen 42:6). He knew them, for, knowing that they too with all the Canaanites were tormented by hunger, as those burned upon a frying-pan, he had been concerned about them even before, saying to himself: “When then will they come to buy bread for themselves?” But seeing his brothers, Joseph made as though he did not know them, “and spoke roughly unto them… ye are spies” (Gen 42:7, 9). The brothers answer: “We do not know the Egyptian tongue, that, using it, we should win the confidence of the Egyptians and spy out their thoughts. We dwell in the land of Canaan, of which thou canst judge from what we have brought hither. Moreover, twelve brothers we are, and we cannot all have one evil wish — to become spies. We came to thee of our own will, and this itself testifies that there is no wrong in us. Since we know not the Egyptian language and our clothing is not Egyptian, it is plain we are not spies. Twelve brothers we are both by birth, and because we are many. We are known everywhere; and behold, one is with our father this day, and one is not” (Gen 42:13).

Joseph, reasoning that his dreams had not yet been fulfilled (for he had seen in a dream that eleven stars bowed down to him, while before him stood only ten brothers), continues to hide himself from them, so as not to make his dreams false. And therefore he says to the brothers: “Then only shall you assure me that you are brothers, when you bring your younger brother to me.” Then he casts them into prison for three days, that they might experience the sorrow which Joseph had endured for many years in confinement.

After this Joseph considers his dreams and remembers that in the dream-vision the brothers had bowed down to him twice — once under the image of sheaves, and once under the image of stars. And from this he concludes that, when the brothers shall bow down to him a second time, then will the time come to reveal himself to them. So he takes Simeon and, in the presence of the other brothers, binds him, in order to learn from him what the brothers told their father about Joseph’s loss. Moreover, he knew that Simeon’s children and his wife would urge Jacob the more quickly to send Benjamin to him. Or it may be that Simeon had shown more cruelty toward Joseph when the brothers bound him and sold him.

Yet one cannot see in this an angry revenge, for when Joseph revealed himself to his brothers, he kissed them with love. But since the one of the brothers who most of all had wished to bind Joseph was now himself bound, they were to see in this a just retribution. Therefore they themselves said: “We are verily guilty, that we deserved this, for we saw the anguish of our brother, when he besought us, and we would not hear” (Gen 42:21). Joseph heard how Reuben spoke this to the brothers and reminded them that he had asked not to cast Joseph into the pit. Then Joseph remembered this and wept; but not because his brothers had so dealt with him, but at the thought from where and to what height God had brought him up.

When Joseph’s brothers, supplied with provisions for the journey, returned home, they told their father of the misfortune that had befallen them on the journey. They told how they had been mocked by the Egyptians, suspected of spying, humiliated, and that now they could be delivered from misfortune only by giving up Benjamin. While some told this to Jacob, others began to untie the sacks, and each found his own silver in his sack. Jacob grieved over what had befallen them, and especially over Simeon, who had been bound. His sons daily entreated him to let Benjamin go with them. But Jacob feared that the same might befall Benjamin as had befallen his brother, and would not consent to their request.
